extracted_information: This document outlines SSL/TLS best practices for 2023, which remain largely relevant for 2024, covering certificate management, HTTPS deployment, and security configurations. It emphasizes the necessity of SSL/TLS for SEO and user trust, even for non-sensitive data.

specifications.TLS_protocol_versions: TLS 1.2 or 1.3 recommended; SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, 1.1 deprecated/disabled

items: Choose a Reliable Certificate Authority (CA): Look for CAs focused on publicly trusted PKI, responsive to vulnerabilities, offering useful products (EV certs, API/ACME issuance, lifecycle management), and good customer support.
Implement Certificate Authority Authorization (CAA): Configure CAA records to designate permitted CAs for your domain.

Generate and Secure Private Keys: Use strong keys (2048-bit RSA or 256-bit ECDSA), generate on secure/trusted environment (server, FIPS/Common Criteria compliant device), never allow CA to generate unless on secure hardware (non-exportable), limit access, revoke all certs for old keys when access changes, renew certificates frequently (yearly preferred) with freshly generated keys, use automation (ACME protocol), revoke immediately if compromised.
Install Complete Certificate Chains: Ensure all intermediate certificates are installed to provide a complete certification path.
Cover all hostnames: Ensure certificates cover domain with/without 'www' and include Subject Alternative Names (SAN) for all protected domain names.
Use Current SSL/TLS Protocols: Prioritize TLS 1.3 (latest) or TLS 1.2 (widely used). Deprecate/disable SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1.
Use a Short List of Secure Cipher Suites: Choose ciphers offering at least 128-bit encryption, move away from DES/variants to AES, and minimize acceptable suites to reduce attack surface.

Use Forward Secrecy (FS) / Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS): For TLS 1.2, configure Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (EDCHE) with DHE fallback, avoid RSA key exchange. TLS 1.3 provides FS via Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (EDH or DHE).
Enable TLS Session Resumption: Allows web servers to reuse negotiated SSL/TLS sessions, bypassing key negotiation overhead for performance.
Consider OCSP Stapling: Deliver cached revocation information directly to client, improving performance by eliminating client OCSP server requests.

Eliminate Mixed Content: Ensure all resources (JS, images, CSS) are accessed via HTTPS to prevent browser warnings and SEO issues. Update hard-coded HTTP URLs to HTTPS. Configure Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header to allow only HTTPS resources.
Use Secure Cookies: Set 'Secure' flag for HTTPS transmission, 'HttpOnly' to prevent client-side JS access, and 'SameSite' to restrict cross-site use.
Evaluate Third-Party Code: Vet third-party libraries for security risks, link via HTTPS, and assess benefit vs. risk.

Implement HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS): Enforce HTTPS connections, prevent protocol downgrade attacks and cookie hijacking. Add 'Strict-Transport-Security' header (e.g., 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains'). Consider HSTS preload list submission.
Utilize Server Name Indication (SNI): Host multiple secure websites on the same IP address and port, ensuring server software supports SNI and configuring separate blocks for each site.
Disable TLS Compression: Mitigate CRIME (Compression Ratio Info-leak Made Easy) attack by disabling compression (e.g., 'SSLCompression off' in Apache).
Implement TLS Session Tickets Correctly: Improve performance, but use unique session ticket keys per server (especially with load balancers) and rotate keys regularly.
Enable Secure Renegotiation: Allow new TLS handshakes within a session for key refreshing or encryption level changes securely.
Disable Client-initiated Renegotiation: Prevent DoS attacks by consuming excessive resources.
Stay Alert for New Vulnerabilities: Continuously monitor for new attacks, apply security patches, and stay updated on software developments.
Use diagnostic tools (e.g., SSL Shopper's SSL Checker) to verify certificate installation, expiry, and chain of trust.
Check for security problems like mixed content using online tools or browser developer tools.
Schedule regular checks of SSL/TLS installation and configuration.
